perturbation.original_text: The Agreement shall expire five (5) years from its effective date specified above unless earlier terminated by a Party by giving the other Party thirty (30) days written notice. Termination or expiration of this Agreement shall not relieve the Board Member of any obligation with respect to the Confidential Information disclosed or developed hereunder prior to termination.
perturbation.changed_text: The Board Member agrees that no license under any patent, copyright or other intellectual property right is granted by implication or otherwise to Board Members under this Agreement. The Agreement shall expire five (5) years from its effective date specified above unless earlier terminated by a Party by giving the other Party thirty (30) days written notice. Termination or expiration of this Agreement shall not relieve the Board Member of any obligation with respect to the Confidential Information disclosed or developed hereunder prior to termination.
perturbation.explanation: The termination clause is moved to a paragraph discussing intellectual property, where it is easily overlooked.
perturbation.law_explanation: Obscuring the termination clause alongside a clause concerning IP rights diminishes the visibility of the contract's duration and conditions for termination. This introduces uncertainty regarding the agreement's lifespan, potentially contradicting the intentions of commercial code which addresses reasonable notification and termination of ongoing contractual relationships.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Â§ 28:2â309. Absence of specific time provisions; notice of termination. | D.C. Law Library Â Toggle mobile menu Code of the District of Columbia Â§ 28:2â309. Absence of specific time provisions; notice of termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. ( Dec. 30, 1963, 77 Stat. 646, Pub. L. 88-243, Â§ 1 .) Prior Codifications 1981 Ed., Â§â28:2-309. 1973 Ed., Â§â28:2-309. Uniform Commercial Code Comment Prior Uniform Statutory Provision: Subsection (1)âsee Sections 43(2), 45(2), 47(1) and 48, Uniform Sales Act, for policy continued under this Article; Subsection (2)ânone; Subsection (3)ânone. Changes: Completely different in scope. Purposes of Changes and New Matter: 1. Subsection (1) requires that all actions taken under a sales contract must be taken within a reasonable time where no time has been agreed upon. The reasonable time under this provision turns on the criteria as to âreasonable timeâ and on good faith and commercial standards set forth in Sections 1-203, 1-204 and 2-103. It thus depends upon what constitutes acceptable commercial conduct in view of the nature, purpose and circumstances of the action to be taken. Agreement as to a definite time, however, may be found in a term implied from the contractual circumstances, usage of trade or course of dealing or performance as well as in an express term. Such cases fall outside of this subsection since in them the time for action is âagreedâ
perturbation.justification: The modification repositions the termination clause within a section concerning intellectual property, which obscures its visibility and potentially leads to oversight. According to the Law Explanation, this diminishment of visibility introduces uncertainty. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Â§ 28:2â309, addresses 'notice of termination' and implies the importance of reasonable notification. By obscuring the termination clause, the changed text weakens the guarantee that both parties are reasonably notified of the agreement's duration and conditions, thereby creating a structural flaw that contradicts the intention behind ensuring reasonable notification. This aligns with the 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' definition, where a required legal disclosure (termination clause) is placed in a misleading section.
